payload.name: apply_patch

payload.message:
Expanded the audit-log pagination filters so you can request entries for specific hotels/modules. The query builder now honors the optional fields already present in `AuditLogQueryParams` (`entityType`, `entityId`, `userId`, `userName`, `action`, `module`, `isSystemAction`) in addition to the free-text search, letting you hit `GET /audit-logs?module=Hotel&entityType=Hotel&entityId=<hotelId>` to see the records for a given hotel module (`src/services/AuditLogService.ts:227`).

I didn’t run automated tests; consider exercising the audit-log listing endpoint with the new filters to confirm the results look right.

content.text:
Expanded the audit-log pagination filters so you can request entries for specific hotels/modules. The query builder now honors the optional fields already present in `AuditLogQueryParams` (`entityType`, `entityId`, `userId`, `userName`, `action`, `module`, `isSystemAction`) in addition to the free-text search, letting you hit `GET /audit-logs?module=Hotel&entityType=Hotel&entityId=<hotelId>` to see the records for a given hotel module (`src/services/AuditLogService.ts:227`).

I didn’t run automated tests; consider exercising the audit-log listing endpoint with the new filters to confirm the results look right.

payload.text:
**Planning update for audit oldValue**

I’m thinking through updating the HotelService’s audit logs so the oldValue field correctly captures previous data instead of empty objects. There are multiple update functions like updateHotel and updateRoom, and each currently logs empty oldValues, so I need to check how we can fetch and record the real old data before updates across these. This looks like a multi-step task requiring inspection of how logUpdate is called and where existing data might be retrieved, so I’ll start by searching for "logUpdate" in HotelService to map out the relevant parts.

payload.text:
**Designing helper for old data fetching**

I'm outlining a helper method to safely fetch old entity data before updates for audit logging. This will reduce repeated try/catch code and ensure updates proceed even if fetching old data fails, while logging warnings. Next, I’ll update each update function to use this helper and include the old snapshot in the audit logs.

payload.text:
**Summarizing code changes and next steps**

I’m preparing a final message that highlights the new `getAuditSnapshot` helper at line 101 of `HotelService.ts` and updates to multiple update methods that now load previous data for auditing. I’ll note that no tests were run and suggest verifying the audit functionality by hitting update endpoints, encouraging the user to consider running relevant tests next.

payload.message:
- Added `getAuditSnapshot` to `HotelService` so we can capture a pre-update snapshot without interrupting the flow if the lookup fails (`src/services/HotelService.ts:101`).
- Every update handler now fetches its current entity (e.g., `getHotelById`, `getRoom`, `getSeasonById`, etc.) before saving and feeds that snapshot into `logUpdate`, so `oldValues` reflects real data for hotels, rooms, seasons, pricing configs, commissions, fee configs, surcharge settings, promotions, and hotel configs (`src/services/HotelService.ts:146`, `205`, `353`, `402`, `433`, `464`, `504`, `544`, `591`).

I didn’t run automated tests here; you can hit any update endpoint and then query `/audit-logs` filtered by `module=Hotel` to confirm the new `oldValues`.

content.text:
- Added `getAuditSnapshot` to `HotelService` so we can capture a pre-update snapshot without interrupting the flow if the lookup fails (`src/services/HotelService.ts:101`).
- Every update handler now fetches its current entity (e.g., `getHotelById`, `getRoom`, `getSeasonById`, etc.) before saving and feeds that snapshot into `logUpdate`, so `oldValues` reflects real data for hotels, rooms, seasons, pricing configs, commissions, fee configs, surcharge settings, promotions, and hotel configs (`src/services/HotelService.ts:146`, `205`, `353`, `402`, `433`, `464`, `504`, `544`, `591`).

I didn’t run automated tests here; you can hit any update endpoint and then query `/audit-logs` filtered by `module=Hotel` to confirm the new `oldValues`.

payload.text:
**Designing audit log filtering helper**

I’m sketching out a helper function to filter the audit log old and new values so they only include fields the user actually updated, skipping undefined values and fields not present before. This will keep the logs focused on changes rather than full objects. I’m also considering edge cases like nested data and metadata fields, and planning to integrate this helper into update calls without mutating inputs. Since the work affects several parts, I plan to approach it carefully.

summary.text:
**Designing audit log filtering helper**

I’m sketching out a helper function to filter the audit log old and new values so they only include fields the user actually updated, skipping undefined values and fields not present before. This will keep the logs focused on changes rather than full objects. I’m also considering edge cases like nested data and metadata fields, and planning to integrate this helper into update calls without mutating inputs. Since the work affects several parts, I plan to approach it carefully.

payload.text:
**Ensuring audit logs exclude system-set fields**

I'm sorting out how to exclude system-set fields like `updatedBy` from audit logs by cloning the update request before adding those fields, so only user-provided changes get recorded. This means capturing a copy of the update data before mutating it, then passing that clean snapshot to `buildAuditChanges`. I'll apply this pattern across update functions to keep audit data accurate.

summary.text:
**Ensuring audit logs exclude system-set fields**

I'm sorting out how to exclude system-set fields like `updatedBy` from audit logs by cloning the update request before adding those fields, so only user-provided changes get recorded. This means capturing a copy of the update data before mutating it, then passing that clean snapshot to `buildAuditChanges`. I'll apply this pattern across update functions to keep audit data accurate.

payload.text:
**Summarizing final response details**

I'll highlight how the updated helper records only user-provided fields, mention specific file paths with line numbers for changes, and explain cloning the payload before adding metadata. I’ll also note that no tests have been run yet.

payload.message:
- Added `buildAuditChanges` next to the existing snapshot helper (`src/services/HotelService.ts:101`). It filters the old/new payloads down to only the keys the client actually sent (ignoring undefined entries) so audit entries stay focused on the user’s changes.
- Each update method now clones the incoming DTO before adding `updatedBy`, computes the diff via `buildAuditChanges`, and logs only those subsets (`src/services/HotelService.ts:165`, `225`, `374`, `424`, `456`, `488`, `529`, `570`, `618`). That way both `oldValues` and `newValues` contain just the fields the user touched, paired with their previous values when available.

I didn’t run automated tests; if you have audit-log fixtures or can hit any update endpoint and query `/audit-logs`, that would validate the new behavior.

summary.text:
**Summarizing final response details**

I'll highlight how the updated helper records only user-provided fields, mention specific file paths with line numbers for changes, and explain cloning the payload before adding metadata. I’ll also note that no tests have been run yet.

content.text:
- Added `buildAuditChanges` next to the existing snapshot helper (`src/services/HotelService.ts:101`). It filters the old/new payloads down to only the keys the client actually sent (ignoring undefined entries) so audit entries stay focused on the user’s changes.
- Each update method now clones the incoming DTO before adding `updatedBy`, computes the diff via `buildAuditChanges`, and logs only those subsets (`src/services/HotelService.ts:165`, `225`, `374`, `424`, `456`, `488`, `529`, `570`, `618`). That way both `oldValues` and `newValues` contain just the fields the user touched, paired with their previous values when available.

I didn’t run automated tests; if you have audit-log fixtures or can hit any update endpoint and query `/audit-logs`, that would validate the new behavior.
